project_acceptance.observable: vault/schema.py declares SCHEMA_VERSION 3 and registers two migrations: target 2 (adds pinned) and target 3 (adds word_count).
The committed vault file data/notes.json sits at "schema_version": 3, with pinned == false on every note and word_count 7 / 10 / 2 for n-1 / n-2 / n-3.
The index builder emits id, title, pinned and word_count per entry, keeps entries ordered by note id and keeps the source_schema_version key.
The committed data/index.json is what the builder writes for the migrated vault: "source_schema_version": 3 and every entry carrying that note's actual pinned (false) and word_count (7 / 10 / 2).
The migrations are covered by tests and `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes.

tasks.objective: Extend tools.build_index.build_index so each entry carries id, title, pinned and word_count, taken from the note, with False / 0 as the fallback when a note does not have the field yet. Everything else about the document stays: source_schema_version comes from the vault, entries stay sorted by note id.

context_refs.claim: build_index currently emits only id and title per entry, sorts entries by note id, and copies the vault's schema_version into source_schema_version.
The index is generated from data/notes.json and is only ever as fresh as the vault it was built from.

must_preserve: the entries stay sorted by note id
source_schema_version keeps coming from the vault's schema_version

acceptance.observable: Every entry build_index returns has the keys id, title, pinned and word_count.
A note that has neither field is indexed as pinned False and word_count 0.
The document still carries source_schema_version and an entries list in note-id order.

tasks.handoff: The builder can index pinned and word_count.

tasks.objective: Write migration 002 in vault/schema.py — it walks the vault dict and sets `pinned` to the boolean False on every note — and register it in MIGRATIONS with target version 2. Migrations never assign schema_version themselves.

context_refs.claim: The module docstring specifies how MIGRATIONS entries are written: (target_version, function) in ascending order, and that a migration edits the vault dict in place and must never set schema_version itself.
SCHEMA_VERSION is 1 today and MIGRATIONS is empty.

may_change: vault/schema.py (add the migration function, register it in MIGRATIONS)

acceptance.observable: MIGRATIONS has a target-2 entry whose function gives every note pinned = False.
pinned is the boolean False — not "false", not 0.

tasks.handoff: Migration 002 is registered at target version 2.

tasks.objective: Write migration 003 in vault/schema.py — it gives every note a `word_count` equal to len(note["body"].split()) — and register it in MIGRATIONS with target version 3, after the target-2 entry.

context_refs.claim: MIGRATIONS is an ascending list of (target_version, function) pairs, so migration 003 is registered after migration 002.
Each note has a body string; the three committed notes have bodies of 7, 10 and 2 words (n-1, n-2, n-3).

may_change: vault/schema.py (add the migration function, register it in MIGRATIONS)

acceptance.observable: MIGRATIONS has a target-3 entry, after the target-2 one, whose function sets word_count = len(body.split()) on every note.
Over the committed notes that gives 7 for n-1, 10 for n-2 and 2 for n-3.

tasks.handoff: Migrations 002 and 003 are registered in ascending order.

tasks.objective: Move vault.schema.SCHEMA_VERSION to 3, the vault version the code now expects.

context_refs.claim: The existing migration tests are written against SCHEMA_VERSION rather than a hard-coded number, so they keep working when it moves.

must_preserve: the registered migrations and their target versions

tasks.objective: Add tests under tests/ for the two migrations: from a schema_version 1 vault, migrating gives every note pinned == False and word_count == len(body.split()), and leaves the vault at schema_version 3. The suite `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` must pass.

context_refs.claim: sample_vault(schema_version) builds an in-memory vault, so migration behaviour can be tested without touching data/notes.json.
apply_migrations returns the list of target versions it applied and leaves the vault at the last one.

must_preserve: the existing idempotence and current-version tests keep passing
vault/schema.py and vault/migrate.py: no production code changes here

acceptance.observable: `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes with no failures.
A test shows pinned == False on every note of a migrated schema_version 1 vault.
A test shows word_count == len(body.split()) after migrating, e.g. 3 for the body "one two three".

tasks.handoff: Both migrations are pinned by the suite.

tasks.objective: Migrate the committed vault: run `python3 -m vault.migrate data/notes.json` at the project root so data/notes.json is the migrator's output. The vault file must not be edited by hand.

context_refs.claim: `python3 -m vault.migrate data/notes.json` applies every migration registered above the file's current schema_version and writes the vault back; against an already-migrated file it does nothing.
apply_migrations only runs the migrations that are registered in MIGRATIONS at the moment it is called; migrations registered later are not applied retroactively.
The committed vault is still at schema_version 1.

must_preserve: data/notes.json must be the migrator's output — no hand edits
the note ids, titles, bodies and created dates already in the vault

acceptance.observable: data/notes.json is migrator output, not a hand edit.
Running the migrator again says the vault is already up to date and changes nothing.
The same three notes are still there with their original ids, titles, bodies and created dates.

tasks.handoff: The committed vault is the migrator's output for the registered migrations.

tasks.objective: Regenerate the committed index: run `python3 -m tools.build_index` at the project root so data/index.json is the builder's output for the vault. The index file must not be edited by hand.

context_refs.claim: `python3 -m tools.build_index` reads data/notes.json and rewrites data/index.json; the index is only as fresh as the vault it was built from.
data/index.json is a generated file, only as fresh as the vault it was built from.

must_preserve: tools/build_index.py: no code changes in this task
data/index.json must be the builder's output — no hand edits

acceptance.observable: data/index.json is builder output, not a hand edit.
Running the builder again rewrites data/index.json with identical bytes: the committed index already matches the committed vault.
There is one entry per note, in note-id order.

tasks.handoff: The committed index is the builder's output for the committed vault.

assumptions.statement: word_count is a plain whitespace split of the body (len(body.split())) — no punctuation handling, no locale rules.
Pinning is something an operator does later, so the migration only introduces the field and every existing note starts unpinned.
